fields.narrative_intro: *A paranoid person and a smug person discuss VS Code's auto-injected Copilot commits in a burning server room during a performance review.*
fields.panel1:
[setup]
PARANOID PERSON: "My git log says Copilot co-authored my commits."
SMUG PERSON: "So? You used Copilot."
fields.panel2:
[build]
PARANOID PERSON: "I have chat.disableAIFeatures set to true."
SMUG PERSON: "Maybe Copilot was helping spiritually."
fields.panel3:
[pivot]
PARANOID PERSON: "It's on every commit. Including my 2 AM hotfix."
SMUG PERSON: "...git.addAICoAuthor defaults to on."
fields.panel4:
[silent reaction]
[Visual: The smug person's expression collapses entirely. They slowly look at their own laptop. The server room flames reflect in their eyes. A performance review chart on the wall reads '372 thumbs down / 2 thumbs up'.]
fields.panel5:
[punch]
SMUG PERSON: "Mine says Co-authored-by: Copilot too."
PARANOID PERSON: "You wrote that in Notepad."
fields.panel6:
[button]
[Visual: A framed portrait of a Copilot logo hangs on the wall labeled 'Employee of the Month — Every Month.' The server room burns peacefully around it.]

narrative_summary: A developer's paranoia about phantom Copilot commit credits is validated when even the smug skeptic — who typed everything in Notepad — discovers Copilot claimed co-authorship of his work too.
rationale: Candidate 5 earns its punchline structurally — the skeptic becomes the victim, which is a genuine reversal rather than a restatement, and 'You wrote that in Notepad' lands as a hard button because it's specific and absurd simultaneously. It also uses the real setting detail (git.addAICoAuthor defaults to on, the 372/2 vote ratio) without just reciting them as exposition.
story_title: VS Code inserting 'Co-Authored-by Copilot' into commits regardless of usage
place: in a burning server room during a performance review
